sentences.text: Yes.
She was buried on the cemetery.
This-- that one was a Jewish cemetery there.
And they just took her out in a cart.
Yes.
Yes.
Ah-ha.
Mm-hm.
Right.
Mm-hm.
No.
I don't think so.
No.
There was no one.
Yeah.
Right.
Mm-hm.
Well, we were working, you know, every--
--everybody was assigned to work.
I was-- I-- I worked in a knitting factory-- no.
It wasn't a factory.
It-- it was-- they teach us, like a school.
It was a school.
And then we had the teachers.
And the younger people came in there, the ones that knew how to knit.
Knitting.
Knitting.
Yeah.
Then we when knitting those little hats for the Germans for [NON-ENGLISH], for the soldiers to cover-- cover their ears-- this.
I don't know.
Some-- that's through the-- through that border there.
Everything came in.
There were people that were in charge of everything, you know.
It was like a little government--
--a little Jewish government.
You know, with money?
Yeah.
We were paid.
I don't remember what.
I don't remember.
It was the--
--ghetto money.
Ghetto money.
Yeah.
Well, you could buy-- I don't know-- a little extra something, maybe besides that ration.
At the beginning-- see, I-- this is something is not very clear--
--with me.
It's not the-- clear how it went. 'Cause, you know, we were so sheltered.
My parents weren't-- you had to have [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what that means?
You have to know people so you would have a broader idea of what's going on, and get some more things.
And our-- my parents didn't.
I mean--
--they did not.
They just knew how to go to work and come back.
And we never had any extras-- ever.
Our family never had any extra.
Where others-- other families had some good food and somehow organized--
--organized things.
They knew--
--the system.
That's exactly, exactly.
No.
No.
No.
We didn't do anything-- just read.
We had a book here and there, and we read.
And we were tired after work.
Cold-- wintertime was unbearably cold-- no heating-- no heating whatsoever, you know.
The-- the-- the walls were all with ice inside the walls-- icy.
Everything was ice.
And-- no.
I don't remember socializing-- just sitting, and worrying, and talking.
Yeah, the-- yeah-- and then meeting a few people we knew at the beginning.
But later on, everybody was just involved with themselves, with fears, and hunger, and pain.
And that's-- and that what it was.
It wasn't-- I-- I don't know.
They had a little social life too, they said.
Later, people told me some theories, you know.
I don't remember.
No.
Yes-- I mean, to a certain time.
In 1942, when the Germans needed a lot of people to dispose of-- so they made selections.
And mine-- they stopped bringing in food to the ghetto.
So people didn't get anything.
And potatoes were waiting on the border there.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
The Germans said [NON-ENGLISH].
And this was our borderline.
And all the food came through that place.
Everything was standing there for two weeks.
The potatoes got all rotten.
And when this selection was over, we all-- I mean, we were ordered, all of us, to come down to the yard, line up.
And they-- and they were selecting us.
Whoever they want on the truck and whom they wanted to stay, put you to the right-- same thing like in Auschwitz, you know.
So, of course, my mother was taken away at the selection.
My father, we sent out to another street, because he was all swollen from hunger.
I have a book here I-- I wanna show you-- the person I'm gonna talk about now.
So anyway, they took my mother away.
And me and my sister went upstairs crying, crying.
We knew this is it.
All of the sudden, my mother comes up to the house.
I said, what happened?
My mother's friend's son was the Jewish police-- in the Jewish police.
And he was assigned to our apartment house to come to help the Nazis, the Germans.
So what he did, he took her out from the line-- from the line of-- from the truck.
He risked his life.
And he put her back in the line again.
And she passed the second time, OK?
So-- so that's what happened.
I bought a book in that Holocaust Museum, and here he is that wonderful guy that I'm so proud of.
And I would like to show it--
--to you later.
He is in this book.
No.
No, he didn't.
In 1942, in October-- October or September.
No.
No, my mother worked somewhere else.
At that time--
--yeah, yeah, yeah.
My mother worked already at that time at-- with the clothes that was coming back-- all the clothes that was coming back from the people that were taken out.
That's where she worked.
But at--
To look over the diamonds, and the gold, and all.
Because they knew people were sewing in things in their garments.
So they were sitting there and selecting, you know.
Children is separately, and adults, and probably sweaters, and all that kind-- and shoes.
So-- but I was gonna tell you about this-- the potatoes.
The potatoes.
Then they came in after it was over, this selection was over, and we were together again-- the four of us.
Everybody got sick-- typhoid, diarrhea.
And everybody got sick.
So my sister and me, we both got very ill.
And we-- we slept.
I had-- I had 'dysenteria'-- 'dysen-terry'-- dysentery?
Yeah.
And hers turned out to be typhoid.
And she couldn't make it.
And she didn't make it.
She died.
And hundreds and thousands of young people were putting piles of bodies. 'Cause I went into the mortuary, and I saw piles and piles of bodies-- oh, in the thousands.
But I was lucky.
I just-- mine-- we drank from the same glass, you know-- both of us.
And so this is when I lost my sister.
And then--
Yeah, yeah, but just-- just threw them in, yeah.
Yeah-- no, no, no, no, no-- separate-- separate.
But just threw them in on a board.
There were no other things to do.
And-- and-- and then my father, we didn't tell my father that she passed away for a while, because he was ill.
At that time he got ill.
And so he-- he suffered.
In-- in 1943, in July, he passed away.
He had, what do you call?
TB-- TB-- and they couldn't-- nothing could be done.
The doctor told us-- the Jewish-- a ghetto doctor-- if the war ends, he has a chance.
And if it doesn't, so-- he can't make it.
Yeah, with no-- they couldn't do nothing.
Yeah.
Mm-hm.
Mm-hm.
And he was also in a so-called hospital.
And then they send him home, and he choked to death.
It was summer-- one little room, no air-- absolutely no air.
It was a horrible, horrible thing to watch-- horrible for me.
Anyway, so that was in '43.
And-- and then, let's see.
Me and my mother.
And then we--
Yeah.
I was still working.
I worked at another-- I worked at this-- it was called, in German, [GERMAN].
[GERMAN].
This was a place where we used to pack the parts of-- to warm up the engines for the planes-- so that part.
And we worked in a-- in a-- I would packing.
I was putting in, you know?
Assembly line-- assembly line, right.
Jewish.
Jewish.
All Jewish people everywhere.
Everything was done by Jewish people.
Mm-hmm.
Well, some were very kind.
Some were nice.
And some were not.
Because everybody wanted to survive.
Even that Rumkowski-- Rumkowski was our-- our-- the eldest of the juden.
And he always appealed to the Jews-- don't steal.
Don't take.
Well, he took himself.
But-- [LAUGHS]
I think about 150,000, 130,000, I would say.
A lot of people came from Germany.
You know, they brought in Germans and Czechoslovakian people.
You know, 'cause we already had our treatment.
And they came.
They look so good.
And they had all the beautiful clothes on.
And my heart was going out for these people.
I really--
No.
[CRYING]
Yeah.
It wasn't empty.
We still had-- because of these people that came in--
--kind of.
But-- but I think we had 80,000, while-- before the big-- the big-- before they closed the ghetto.
So they brought out about 80,000. 700 supposed to be saved, I mean, that's in the books--
--I mean, that they write about.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
No.
No.
I don't.
No.
No.
Nothing.
No, I just went to work, and-- I don't.
And no more work--
--no more work-- after '43, no.
I don't think we were working.
No.
It was worse, because they-- they give you that little food.
What you got was hardly enough to survive.
And people that worked still had some extra money to buy extra.
Yeah.
No.
I wasn't that type.
[LAUGHTER] No.
No, I never-- well, you couldn't-- a large ghetto, you couldn't.
You could never.
It was too watched-over.
I mean, very seldom, I don't think any-- you approached it only-- you know, you-- you got killed.
You close to that border.
To the gate it was-- it was a--
No.
Upstairs-- I mean, what you call?
A bridge-- a bridge-- a bridge, yeah.
No.
I don't think so.
I-- I don't remember--
--working.
I just don't remember.
Day after day, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --you know, we had neighbors.
And we visited and talked to neighbors, you know-- and hoping that it's going to get better.
You know, and then people gave up.
I mean, nobody--
--was positive, you know?
Nobody was positive anymore.
I stayed in the ghetto till 1944, a year later.
It was like 11 months, you know, after my father died.
No.
No, it was July-- I think the beginning of July.
That's what I think it was, July.
There were-- there was supposed-- when-- in-- in 1944, there was selections were taking place every few months.
And we all had to be in it-- all of us.
They said, if they find you, they're going to kill you-- if they find you in a-- in a room.
So they also, in-- in-- in '43, they took away my aunt, my uncle, and my cousins-- young cousins, you know, and my mother's brothers.
The whole family went.
And we didn't know where they did-- where they took them.
But they were taken out.
So-- Had you stayed in touch with them in the ghetto?
In the ghetto, yeah.
But nobody was that close.
I mean, it's just-- I often think about it.
Nobody-- everybody was just busy with their own--
--serious, you know, survival and hunger, you know.
I mean, even when my father died, nobody came.
Me and my mother-- that's the only thing-- we went to the funeral, you know?
But then, it's 1944, and my sister's girlfriend begs me to go with, because they were selected to go-- to be sent out from the ghetto.
You know, they promise-- the promises were that they're going to-- you going to work.
You're gonna have it good.
You're gonna-- so they were excited, you know-- some of the people I knew.
And she said, Rose, you must go with us.
I have a mother, you have a mother.
We have to go, you know.
And by that time, my mother was selected to go too.
Had been selected, yeah-- to go-- to go too.
And I was hiding.
I was hiding out in my neighbor's house.
But I told you about that.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
So I was-- I was.
And by the time the selection was over, I come down to my room, and my mother is not there.
So I knew already, you know, where she is.
And the same policeman-- our friend, came over.
And he told me.
You know, your mother was taken.
I said, you know what?
I'm gonna take my-- I took my little-- what do you call-- back-- backpack, and I ran to that-- to the wagons where all the Jews were in already.
And I-- the German, the Nazi, called each-- each compartment to see where my mother is so I could be with her together.
When you're talking about wagons and compartments, are they-- are you talking about the train already?
Or were these the carts taking them to the--
The train.
The train.
She was already at the train station.
But what I was gonna say-- I'm-- I'm mixing a little up now.
I'm mixing a little.
What happened is that-- that-- that a girl asked me to go, and I did not.
So my little ration card and mother's was taken away.
Because we did not come at that time to the place to be taken away.
So they did not feed us at all.
This was before.
Yeah, this was with--
--when the friend--
Yeah.
Before.
So my friend, when she begged me to go with her-- right?
OK.
So I-- and my intuition said no.
You're not going.
So I was hiding for two weeks-- mother and me.
Mother went at night somewhere else.
I went somewhere else, you know, wherever we could.
And-- because the Jewish police was looking for you.
See, they had your names.
And-- and I told her, no.
I'm not going.
Anyway, she did go.
And after the war, I found out where they were taken-- to Chelmno.
Everybody was-- everybody was finished up there-- finished off there.
And my intuition said no.
So the last day I couldn't anymore.
Almost, I almost was ready to go.
And all of the sudden, I hear voices in the streets.
People are so happy.
They stopped the selections.
They are not taking people out anymore.
You know?
So it was great.
But what happened, they decided to take everybody.
They decided to close the ghetto.
And this is when, you know, I was saved.
And in Auschwitz, at least you had a choice-- a chance.
I had a chance.
So here I am-- and my mother too.
To look.
This was-- this was two weeks before--
--when the others left.
The others--
Yeah.
That's right.
I did not go.
So my intuition told me, you are not going.
And that's what happened.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Oh, just was one box, one box train, you know, with who knows how many people.
We couldn't-- we had to stand.
You couldn't even sit.
And with the buckets-- the buckets of-- of all the waste right there, coming in, out, and out, and in.
And it was-- I mean, we still didn't know where we going.
We still--
It took off.
It didn't stay long.
It took off, yeah.
It took off.
Ah, I wouldn't know that.
I don't know.
I don't know, really.
No, no, no-- not days-- no, no, no-- hours.
Yeah, hours.
And-- and when I arrived there, and I looked out through a hole, it was a funny way how I felt.
And I said to my mother, this is not for us-- when we arrived at Auschwitz.
I looked out, and I saw people, you know-- dark black.
I thought they're gypsies, because they sent in gypsies to the ghetto one time-- at one time-- not with us, but adjacent to us.
They send them in, and-- and then they said that they took them out to kill.
This is-- that-- those were the rumors at the time.
And so I said, oh.
These are the gypsies here.
It's not for us.
We're not going out here.
Look at that.
This is not a place for us.
And sure enough, a second later, the doors open like a-- like-- like an-- ugh-- with such a bang-- and the screams, and the yells.
[GERMAN]!
Out, out, out!
And you couldn't even concentrate where you are and what's going on.
By the time I knew, I was walking.
I don't know what happened, you know.
You just couldn't-- couldn't understand what's going on.
So I was walking.
We walked.
My mother, yeah.
She was lucky.
She was lucky at this-- this time too.
And-- and we came in to this place where they were shaving us.
And they were, you know, bathing, us, and giving you, you know, like one-- one person got the top of a pajama, the other person got the bottom of the pajama.
You know, and this took till late at night when it was dark.
And we came in, and-- and then they let us go out.
I wouldn't know where I'm going and what we are doing.
Everybody's crying.
Nobody recognizes anybody anymore, because all the hair is gone, you know?
And people change.
They see environments like that, they-- you-- so a sister couldn't recognize a sister.
And they brought us into one place.
It was dark.
And I touched, and I touched, and I touched.
Where are we?
So I found a hole.
And I touched again, and there's another hole.
So later on I learned this was the bathroom for 1,000 people.
They had this bathroom, the holes, for so many people to come in.
And there they kept us for a little while until they brought us in to the barracks there.
So there was a-- a girl, you know, she was a neighbor of mine.
And I knew her.
But she has been-- had been there already for two years.
And everybody cries, where are we?
What-- she said, don't ask questions.
I said-- she said to us, you are the lucky ones.
The others, you see that-- you see that smoke?
That's what happened to the others.
You know, you talk about it-- it was at night and it was so dark.
When you got to the barracks, were there any lights there at all?
No, no, no.
I didn't see any lights.
I don't-- I don't remember how long we stayed in this one and when-- when they brought us out.
This, I don't remember it through the barracks.
And so we-- we--
Yeah, to the right.
I mean, it was in such a manner.
I still see the guy, the Nazi.
You know, I don't know who he was.
But he just pushed me down there and pushed my mother down there, you know, in such a hurry, in such a-- they were so nervous themselves.
And so-- and then we were at the barracks, you know.
And we learned.
And here you got the little spoon.
And you got the little dish to hold onto.
And-- and here I meet my two friends.
They were in another barrack.
And I said to them like, why don't you join me?
I said, you come move with so we'll be together-- so naive, I mean.
[LAUGHS] I still think about that, you know-- when I told them.
And we talk about it.
One is still alive, you know.
She lives in Sweden.
And-- [LAUGHS] --come and join us.
We'll be together.
Because we grew up together in Poland, you know.
We're close.
And then-- and then that was-- Birkenau, actually.
They took us to Birkenau.
I was only there for two weeks-- two weeks--
--of terror-- terror.
Well, look.
We-- we sit at these-- on these bunks-- eight people on this small little thing.
And they would throw at us one dish of soup for seven-- for seven, for eight, whatever.
I still remember that.
And-- and you couldn't move to the bathroom.
You have to wait till they were ready to take you.
And then early in the morning, you stood on appell, and-- or you had to knee down, knell--
Kneel down.
Kneel down.
Kneel down, yeah, for hours in the mud, in the dirt.
This was '44.
Season?
It was in July.
July.
Yeah.
It was hot, yeah-- and-- and thirst-- people were thirsty.
I was thirsty.
I couldn't eat.
I was so hungry in the ghetto.
And over there, they gave you a nice piece of bread, a nice piece of sausage.
And I just couldn't eat.
I did not eat.
I couldn't eat.
I stopped eating-- only water, water, water.
